  "opinion": "As a retired man from Washington DC who spends only a few times a month on social media, I've seen how quickly misinformation spreads. My background with upper secondary education and a lifetime of learning from my parents' experiences taught me to be skeptical. When I do check social media, I focus on the basics: verify sources by checking official websites or trusted news outlets, avoid sharing unconfirmed stories, and look for multiple perspectives. I remember my father telling me to 'question everything' back in the day, and that advice still holds true. Since I'm not overly active online, I don't get caught up in the drama of viral falsehoods. My high conscientiousness helps me stay organized and deliberate in my approach—no impulse reactions to posts. I also enjoy cycling and football, which keep me grounded in real-world interactions rather than digital chaos. I've never been a big fan of viral trends, so I don't get distracted by fake news. When I do encounter something questionable, I take a moment to think it through, just as I would with a complex football strategy or a tricky knitting pattern. My moderate neuroticism means I don't panic but do stay alert. It's important to remember that not everyone is as tech-savvy as younger generations, so simplicity is key. My advice is to be patient, verify facts, and prioritize real-life connections over online noise.",

response_parsed.topic: How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.

persona.time_onsocialmedia: You spend a few times monthly on social media

response_parsed.opinion: As a retired man from Washington DC who spends only a few times a month on social media, I've seen how quickly misinformation spreads. My background with upper secondary education and a lifetime of learning from my parents' experiences taught me to be skeptical. When I do check social media, I focus on the basics: verify sources by checking official websites or trusted news outlets, avoid sharing unconfirmed stories, and look for multiple perspectives. I remember my father telling me to 'question everything' back in the day, and that advice still holds true. Since I'm not overly active online, I don't get caught up in the drama of viral falsehoods. My high conscientiousness helps me stay organized and deliberate in my approach—no impulse reactions to posts. I also enjoy cycling and football, which keep me grounded in real-world interactions rather than digital chaos. I've never been a big fan of viral trends, so I don't get distracted by fake news. When I do encounter something questionable, I take a moment to think it through, just as I would with a complex football strategy or a tricky knitting pattern. My moderate neuroticism means I don't panic but do stay alert. It's important to remember that not everyone is as tech-savvy as younger generations, so simplicity is key. My advice is to be patient, verify facts, and prioritize real-life connections over online noise.
response_parsed.reasoning_summary: Start by analyzing persona traits: retired, Washington DC resident, upper secondary education, low openness (29), high conscientiousness (75), high extraversion (100), moderate agreeableness (56), moderate neuroticism (66). Focus on practical, non-technical advice aligned with a person who doesn't use social media frequently. Use examples from hobbies (cycling, football) to illustrate critical thinking. Emphasize verification, skepticism, and real-world grounding. Avoid complex jargon to match the persona's educational background. Ensure the tone is calm and experienced, reflecting a 85-year-old man with a normal psychological profile. Mention the importance of family and community to align with marital status and children. Keep the opinion between 250-500 words, written in continuous prose without bullet points.
